states.text: "Shrine discipline means preparing before the fight begins. Keep the prayer circle ready, keep spell points in reserve, and do not spend your final blessing on a bruise. The faithful who live longest are usually the ones who planned ahead."

"When you call greater heal, you are asking the shrine to bind the wounded back together before despair can take hold. Use it on the ally who keeps the rest alive, not on the first person to complain."

params.text: The shrine mentor taught me to prepare before battle, conserve spell points, and reserve greater heal for the ally the fight cannot afford to lose.

states.text: A young stablehand leans on a pitchfork, straw clinging to her sleeves. Horses stamp and snort in the stalls behind her. "If you are looking for a mount, you have come to the right place. If you are looking for advice on the roads, I hear more from the riders than anyone in town."

transitions.text: Tell me about the mounts you have.
What have riders been saying about the roads?

params.text: The stablehand shared road conditions: the northern pass is clear, the eastern marsh road is dangerous after rain, and the southern trade road sees heavy bandit activity at night.

transitions.text: Here is something for your trouble.

states.text: "We have draft horses for cargo, riding horses for speed, and a couple of warhorses the garrison left behind. Draft horses are slow but sure-footed — good for mountain roads. Riding horses are fast on open ground but spook at wolves. The warhorses do not spook at anything, but they eat twice as much."

"The northern pass is clear this time of year. The eastern marsh road floods after heavy rain — riders have been turning back or losing shoes in the mud. The southern trade road is fine by day, but the caravans are reporting bandits working the stretch between the bridge and the crossroads after dark."

transitions.text: I will plan my route carefully.

states.text: "Much appreciated. If you are headed east, tell the farrier at the crossroads I sent you — he will check your mount's shoes for half price. And water your horse before you leave. The next well is a long ride."

A broad-shouldered stable master inspects a horse's leg with practiced hands, barely glancing up. "I manage every mount in this garrison. Sixty head, all fed, shod, and fit for duty. State your business — I do not have time for idle chat."

transitions.text: What can you tell me about the garrison's mounts?
Can you assess my mount's condition?
How should I prepare a mount for long travel?
I will not take more of your time.

states.text: "We keep warhorses for the cavalry, draft horses for the supply wagons, and a handful of fast couriers for dispatch riders. Every animal is inspected weekly. A lame horse in the field means a dead soldier. I do not tolerate sloppy care."

"Hooves are worn — needs reshoeing before any mountain terrain. The coat is dull, which means the feed has been poor. Switch to oats with a handful of salt and the condition will improve within a week. Otherwise, sound legs and good wind. Ten gold for the assessment."

transitions.text: I will get the shoes done.

params.text: The stable master assessed my mount: needs reshoeing before mountain travel, and better feed (oats with salt) to improve coat condition.

states.text: "Shoe the horse fresh before any long ride. Carry extra feed — you cannot count on grazing in the wilds. Water at every stream. And check the hooves morning and evening for stones. A stone bruise will lame a horse faster than a sword cut."

A farrier hammers a glowing horseshoe on an anvil set beside the road, sparks flying with each strike. A patient mare stands tethered nearby. "Need shoes? A trim? Or just directions? I shoe every horse that passes this crossroads, so I know where they all come from and where they are going."

"A good shoe lasts two months on packed roads, less on stone. Mountain terrain chews through iron fast — if you are headed for the peaks, get the shoes checked at every stop. I use a slightly wider shoe for mountain work. Costs the same, lasts longer on uneven ground."

transitions.text: What is happening at the crossroads?

states.text: "Done. Fresh shoes all around. That will hold for a good two months unless you are climbing scree. Eight gold. Check them every few days — if you hear a click on stone, one is coming loose."

transitions.text: I will listen for the click.

states.text: "Caravans from the south are running late — the bridge toll doubled last week and half the merchants are taking the long way around. A courier came through this morning looking rattled, said something about wolves denning near the eastern ford. And the garrison sent a patrol north, but they have not come back yet."

transitions.text: I will keep my eyes open.

params.text: The farrier at the crossroads reported: caravans delayed by doubled bridge tolls, wolves denning near the eastern ford, and a garrison patrol overdue from the north.

states.text: A dusty courier drops a leather satchel on the counter, pulling a water flask from her belt. "Just rode in from the eastern settlements. If you need a message delivered or want to know what is happening beyond the walls, I am your fastest source of information."

transitions.text: Which routes are safe right now?

states.text: "The mining camps are dealing with sickness — some kind of plague in the water. The herbalists are overwhelmed. The mountain hold sent word that the elemental wards are holding but they need fresh crystal supplies. And the frontier post reported goblin raiding parties moving west along the ridge."

params.text: Courier report: plague in the mining camps, elemental wards holding but low on crystals at the mountain hold, goblin raiders moving west from the frontier.

states.text: "Fifteen gold for standard dispatch. I will have it there within three days — faster if the weather holds. I ride the northern pass to the mountain hold, then south through the forest haven. No detours, no delays."

"The northern pass is clear and well-patrolled. The eastern marsh road is treacherous — flooded sections and the locals are reporting undead at night. The southern trade road is safe by day but bandits work the bridge after dark. If you must go east, take the ridge trail above the marsh. Longer, but you keep your boots dry and your throat uncut."

transitions.text: The ridge trail. I will remember.

states.text: A caravan broker sits beneath a faded awning, ledger open, quill in hand. Crates and barrels are stacked around the plaza. "I arrange transport, negotiate routes, and match cargo with caravans. If you need goods moved or want to ride with a convoy for safety, I am the one to talk to."

transitions.text: When is the next caravan departing?
I need an escort along the trade road.
Which trade routes are most profitable right now?
I will manage on my own.

states.text: "A supply caravan heads to the mountain hold in two days — heavy guard, slow pace, safe as it gets. A fast merchant convoy leaves tomorrow for the coastal fort, but they only take paying passengers. And a herbalist wagon is looking for an armed escort to the forest haven. That one pays you instead of the other way around."

"Twenty gold for a seat on the next guarded convoy along the southern trade road. You ride in the middle, stay alert, and help with the watches. If there is trouble, you fight. If there is not, you arrive in one piece. Fair terms."

"The mountain hold is buying iron and selling gemstones — good margin if you can haul ore uphill. The coastal fort pays well for dried meat and grain — the fishing boats do not feed the whole garrison. And the desert outpost will take water and cloth at triple the plains price. Risk and profit go hand in hand."

params.text: The caravan broker shared trade intelligence: iron to the mountain hold for gemstones, grain to the coastal fort, water and cloth to the desert outpost at triple prices.

states.text: A garrison cook stirs an enormous pot of stew, sweat beading on her forehead. The kitchen smells of onions, roast meat, and woodsmoke. "I feed three hundred mouths a day. If you want a meal, get in line. If you want to talk, make it quick — the bread is about to burn."

transitions.text: What is on the menu today?
I brought provisions from my travels.
How are the garrison food supplies holding up?
I will get out of your kitchen.

states.text: "Stew. Same as yesterday, same as tomorrow. Onions, root vegetables, and whatever meat the hunters bring in. On a good day there is bread. On a bad day there is hardtack. The officers get cheese and dried fruit, but do not tell the soldiers that."
"Dried meat! That stretches the stew for two more days. Here — fifteen gold from the quartermaster's fund. If you come across salt or grain on the road, I will pay even better for those."

transitions.text: I will keep an eye out.

states.text: "We have grain for a month, meat for two weeks, and salt for three days. Salt is the real crisis — without it the meat spoils and the stew tastes like dishwater. The last supply caravan was delayed, and the foragers say the game is thinning. If someone does not fix the supply line soon, morale drops before the rations do."

params.text: The garrison cook reported critical salt shortage — three days of supply left. The supply caravan is late and game is thinning. Morale will follow the rations.

states.text: A dock worker sets down a heavy crate and stretches his back, hands on hips. "You are not from the docks. The harbor master is up the stairs, the warehouse is that way, and if you want passage on a ship, talk to the captain at the third berth. Unless you just want gossip — I have plenty of that."

transitions.text: I need directions around the harbor.

params.text: The dock worker gave directions: harbor master upstairs, warehouse to the east, ship passage at the third berth. The tavern closest to the docks is the Salt Barrel.

transitions.text: Need a hand with those crates?
I will find my own way.

states.text: "A ship came in last night flying no flag — the harbor master is pretending he did not notice. Three crates marked for the garrison went missing from the warehouse — the guard is investigating but everyone knows it was the smugglers from the south pier. And the fishermen are saying the catches are smaller every week. Something in the deep water is driving the schools away."

transitions.text: Can you point me around the harbor?
Interesting. I will keep that quiet.

states.text: "The harbor master's office is up the stone stairs to the left. The main warehouse is the big building east of the second pier. For ship passage, the third berth usually has a merchant vessel taking passengers. And if you want a drink and a dry seat, the Salt Barrel tavern is right there at the foot of the dock road."

"You are stronger than you look. That saved me an hour. Here — the foreman keeps a few coins for hired help. Ten gold for the work. And if you need dock work again, we always need hands before the morning tide."

A weathered stablehand brushes down a horse in the yard of a roadside inn, humming a tune. "Travelers' horses always tell me what the road was like. Muddy hooves mean the eastern trail. Dusty means the southern stretch. Bloody means you had a rough night. Which is it?"

transitions.text: Board my mount for the night.

states.text: "Three gold for overnight boarding — feed, water, and a rubdown. I check hooves and look for injuries. If the horse needs shoeing, the farrier at the crossroads is half a day's ride east. For long stays, we do a full grooming. Keeps the flies off and the coat healthy."
"Stall seven, fresh hay and oats. I will have her brushed and checked by morning. If you are leaving early, knock on the stable door twice — I am a light sleeper. Three gold, paid."

transitions.text: Early start. I will knock twice.

states.text: "A merchant came in with a lathered horse — said he was chased by wolves on the eastern approach. Two soldiers rode through heading north in a hurry, would not say why. And a herbalist passed through this morning asking about plague symptoms in the mining camps. The roads are busy and not in a good way."

transitions.text: Busy roads. I will stay alert.

params.text: The roadside stablehand reported: wolves on the eastern approach, soldiers heading north in haste, and a herbalist investigating plague reports from the mining camps.

states.text: A stablehand rubs a saddle strap with lamp oil. "If you ride far, care for the tack before you brag about the road. Need a quick lesson?"

params.text: The stablehand warned me that a neglected harness fails long before the road does.

transitions.text: I can manage my own tack.

states.text: "Dry the straps, brush the grit out, and never leave a wet saddle blanket bundled overnight. Lose the harness and you lose the ride, no matter how brave you feel."
A stablehand checks a feed bucket and glances at your boots. "Road dust on the heels, hungry eyes on the mount. Want to hear how not to ruin an animal in two days?"

transitions.text: Tell me how to keep a mount fit.

states.text: "Water first, feed second, rest whenever you can steal it. A tired beast will carry you into danger and die there, which is bad for both of you."
A quartermaster runs a thumb down a dented ledger. "Most expeditions fail before the first arrow. They fail because nobody counted rope, food, or dry socks. Need the short version?"

params.text: The quartermaster says expeditions fail from neglected supplies long before they fail from heroics.

states.text: "Track what keeps you moving: food, bandages, lamp oil, spare cord, and one tool you hope not to need. If you do not count it before dawn, you will miss it by dusk."
A quartermaster taps a sealed manifest tube against an armored crate. "Escort work is half marching and half remembering what must arrive intact. Want the quartermaster's rule?"

transitions.text: I have heard enough of manifests.

states.text: "Count the cargo at sunrise, count it again after every halt, and never let panic scatter the carriers. People forget crates in a rout; quartermasters do not."

transitions.text: I will keep the line tight.

states.text: A baker pulls hard biscuits from a cooling rack. "Fresh bread is for home. Road bread is for surviving until home exists again. Want to know the difference?"
"Dry bread, salted fat, and something to cut the bitterness. Pack food that forgives neglect. Hungry travelers romanticize feasts, but survivors learn to love plain rations."
A jailer weighs a ring of keys with one tired hand. "Night watch is not glory. It is routine, caution, and checking the same lock until dawn. Want the lesson?"

params.text: The jailer says routine and repeated checks keep small mistakes from becoming disasters.

transitions.text: I will leave you to the locks.

states.text: "Check the hinge, check the latch, check the corridor, and then check your own memory. Trouble loves the place where a tired guard assumes the last look was enough."

A scarred garrison guard leans against the gatehouse wall, arms crossed, watching the road with tired eyes. "Another traveler. The road is open but the garrison is short-handed. If you have business inside, state it. If not, move along."

transitions.text: The garrison looks stretched thin. What happened?
I have fought beside soldiers before. I could use a steady shield at my side.
I will not keep you from your watch.

states.text: "Half the watch rotated south to deal with the marsh trouble. The rest of us cover double shifts. I have been at this gate for sixteen hours. The captain says reinforcements are coming. The captain has been saying that for three weeks."

transitions.text: That is a hard position. Have you thought about leaving?

states.text: "Leave? A soldier does not leave his post. But a soldier whose post has already left him — that is a different question. I have no loyalty to empty promises. But I will not follow someone I do not trust, and I will not follow someone the garrison would not respect."

transitions.text: I have earned respect on the road. Let me prove it. [PRE 14]

states.text: "You carry yourself like someone who has led people and brought them back alive. That counts for something. But words at a gate are cheap. I need to know you have stood your ground when it mattered — that people trust you with their lives, not just their coin."

transitions.text: Ask anyone in this region. My name means something.

params.text: Haldric, the garrison guard, has agreed that I am worth following. He is willing to leave the undermanned garrison and join my company when the time comes.
Haldric respects my bearing but does not yet trust me enough to leave his post. I need to build a stronger reputation before he will consider it.

states.text: "I asked around. The people you helped — they speak well of you. Not flattery, but the quiet kind of trust that soldiers recognize. When you are ready to march, send word. I will be at the gate until then, but my oath will be to you, not to an empty barracks."

transitions.text: I will send word when the time comes.

states.text: A guard with close-cropped hair sits sharpening a blade on the barracks steps, methodical and unhurried. Her shield bears dents from real combat, not training. "You are not garrison. What do you want?"

transitions.text: You look like someone who has seen real fighting, not just gate duty.
What should I know about this area?

states.text: "The eastern road is bad — wolves denning near the ford, and the marsh gives everything a hiding place. The northern pass is clean if you travel by day. If you are headed south, bring company. Bandits work the bridge in pairs now. They are getting organized."

transitions.text: You seem overqualified for wall patrol.

states.text: "I served two years on the frontier before they rotated me to this garrison. The frontier was honest — you fought what came, and you knew who stood beside you. Here it is politics, paperwork, and watching a gate nobody attacks. I did not sign up to rust."

transitions.text: I am building a company for real work. No paperwork, no politics. [PRE 13]
The frontier sounds like it suited you.

states.text: "Real work. I have heard that pitch before — usually from people who get their company killed in the first ambush. I do not follow talk. I follow track records. Prove you can lead and that the people who follow you do not regret it."

transitions.text: Judge me by what you have seen, not what you have heard.

params.text: Senna, the frontier-trained guard, has assessed my leadership and found it sufficient. She is willing to leave garrison duty and join my company when called.

transitions.text: Fair enough. I will prove it.

states.text: "I have watched you handle yourself. You do not flinch, you do not bluff, and you do not leave people behind. That is enough. When you need a shield on your flank, you know where to find me. I will have my kit packed."

transitions.text: I will not waste your time when I call.

states.text: A priest kneels before a modest shrine at the edge of town, hands folded, brow furrowed. His traveling robes are patched and road-worn. "The light finds those who seek it, and sometimes those who do not. I am Brother Aldwyn. What brings you to this place?"

transitions.text: You look like a man carrying more than prayers. What troubles you?
Can you offer healing or blessing?

states.text: "I offer what the light provides. Minor wounds, a blessing for the road, and counsel for the spirit. I do not charge — the light was not given to me so I could sell it. But I accept donations to the shrine if you feel moved."

transitions.text: What weighs on your mind, Brother?

states.text: "The order sent me to tend this shrine, but I have seen the plague in the mining camps, the undead in the marshes, and the suffering on every road between. Tending one shrine while the world burns feels like polishing a candlestick in a collapsing cathedral. I want to do more. But a priest does not abandon his post without cause."

transitions.text: I walk those roads. The people on them need more than shrines — they need someone willing to bring the light to where the darkness is. [PRE 14]
Your duty here matters too, Brother.

states.text: "You speak with conviction, and I sense no greed or cruelty in your words. But conviction alone is not enough. The light teaches discernment — I must know that the cause you serve is worthy, and that you treat those who follow you with the same care you would ask of the divine."

transitions.text: I have stood against the darkness in this region and the people I have helped can vouch for my character.

params.text: Brother Aldwyn has weighed my cause and my character. He is willing to leave his shrine and walk the dangerous roads at my side, bringing healing and the light where they are needed most.

transitions.text: I will continue the work. Perhaps you will see enough to decide.

params.text: Brother Aldwyn respects my intent but needs to see more evidence of my character before he will leave his shrine duties. I should help the region and return.

states.text: "I have prayed on it. The shrine will stand without me — but the people on the road may not stand without the light. When you are ready to march, I will carry my faith beside your banner. Not as a servant, but as a partner in a shared obligation."

transitions.text: A shared obligation. I will honor it.

states.text: A healer with steady hands and a direct gaze sorts dried herbs into labeled jars behind a clinic table. A line of patients waits outside. "If you are wounded, sit down. If you are not wounded, make room for someone who is. I do not have time for pleasantries."

transitions.text: Your clinic is overwhelmed. Where is the town healer?
I brought healing herbs from the road.
I will get out of your way.

states.text: "The town healer retired. His replacement never arrived. So I handle it — cuts, fevers, broken bones, and the plague cases they bring from the mining camps. One person, no assistant, no supplies beyond what I can forage. I manage. But managing is not the same as curing."

transitions.text: You could do more with support. Better supplies, a wider reach.
The town is lucky to have you.

states.text: "Herbs. Real ones, not the wilted stems the merchants try to pass off. You have my attention. These will keep three patients stable through the night. Not many travelers think to bring supplies to a clinic. What kind of person does that?"

transitions.text: The kind who knows a good healer is worth more than a good sword.

states.text: "A wider reach. You mean leave the clinic and travel with you. I have thought about it — a healer on the road saves different lives than a healer behind a desk. But I will not follow someone who sees me as a walking potion. I need to know you value the work, not just the convenience."

transitions.text: I would not ask if I did not respect what you do. The road needs you more than this clinic does. [PRE 13]
I understand. The clinic needs you.

states.text: "Respect is a start. But I have seen people promise respect and then demand I heal their hangover at three in the morning. I watch what people do, not what they say. If the people who know you speak well of your character, I will trust their judgment."

transitions.text: Then ask them. I have nothing to hide.

params.text: Maren, the clinic healer, has verified my reputation and agreed to leave her overwhelmed clinic to serve as field healer in my company. Her skills will be invaluable on the road.

states.text: "I asked. They said you kept your word, you did not leave the wounded behind, and you did not haggle over the cost of doing the right thing. That is enough. I will find someone to cover the clinic. When you are ready, I will have my kit and my jars packed."

transitions.text: The road will be safer with you.

states.text: A scout crouches at the edge of camp, checking a bowstring and counting arrowheads with silent precision. "You walk too loudly. I heard you coming from fifty paces. If you want to talk, sit down and lower your voice. Sound carries."

transitions.text: What do you know about the trails ahead?
I have not seen anyone read terrain the way you do. Where did you learn?
I will try to walk quieter.

states.text: "The eastern trail has wolf sign — fresh scat and claw marks on the birches. Northern pass is clear but exposed. The ridge trail above the marsh is the safest route east if you do not mind the climb. I mapped the game trails last week — the deer are moving west, which means something is pushing them."

transitions.text: That is impressive field intelligence. Who trained you?
I will take the ridge trail. Thank you.

states.text: "I apprenticed under the ranger outpost commander before the outpost was decommissioned. Budget cuts. Now I freelance — trail reports for caravans, pathfinding for merchant convoys, the occasional bounty when a wolf pack gets too bold. It pays, but it is not a purpose. I miss belonging to something that mattered."

transitions.text: I am building something that matters. I need eyes and ears that can read the land before the fight starts. [PRE 12]
The outpost's loss. Your skills are wasted on caravan reports.

states.text: "Something that matters. I have heard that before, usually from people who mean something that matters to them. I am not interested in being someone's disposable pair of eyes. I need to know you value the intelligence I bring, and that you listen when I say the ground is bad."

transitions.text: A scout who is not listened to is a wasted scout. I do not waste people.

params.text: Veris, the freelance scout, has evaluated my leadership and found me worth following. They will serve as my company's eyes and ears when I am ready to call on them.

transitions.text: I hear you. Let me show you I mean it.

states.text: "I watched how you moved through the area. You checked your flanks, you read the ground before crossing open terrain, and you did not rush past the signs I left to test you. You listen. That is enough. When you are ready to move, I will be a half-mile ahead making sure the path is clear."

transitions.text: A half-mile ahead. I will trust your judgment.

states.text: A wiry man in a well-cut but deliberately plain coat sits in the back corner of a tavern, nursing a drink that he has not actually touched. His eyes track every person entering and leaving. "I do not know you. That is unusual — I know everyone who matters in this district. State your interest or move on."

transitions.text: I need information that the official channels do not carry.
What is the real situation in this district?

states.text: "The official story is that the garrison keeps order and the merchants keep commerce flowing. The real story is that the garrison is stretched thin, the merchants are cutting deals with smugglers to keep their margins, and the only thing keeping actual order is the fact that the three criminal families are too busy fighting each other to organize. When that changes, this district burns."

transitions.text: You know a lot about the underside. What is your angle?
Good to know. I will keep my head down.

states.text: "My angle is survival. I facilitate — goods, information, introductions. I do not steal, I do not kill, and I do not betray a client. That is why I am still alive and the last three people who held this seat are not. I have been looking for an exit that does not involve a knife in the dark. But exits require trust, and trust is not something I hand out."

transitions.text: I could use someone who knows the underside of every district I pass through. Information saves more lives than swords. [PRE 15]
I appreciate the honesty. Stay safe.

states.text: "Information saves lives — you understand that. Most sword-swingers do not. But I have survived this long by reading people, and I will not stake my life on a speech. I need to see how the street reacts to you. People who fear you are a liability. People who respect you — that I can work with."

transitions.text: Then watch. I do not hide how I operate.

params.text: Tomas, the district fence, has observed my conduct and decided I am a safer bet than his current position. He is willing to serve as my company's intelligence and logistics specialist when the time comes.

transitions.text: I will give you time to watch.

states.text: "I watched. You paid fair prices, you did not threaten anyone who did not threaten you first, and the people in this district nod when you pass instead of locking their doors. That tells me everything. When you leave this district, I will be two streets behind making sure nobody follows who should not. My kind of loyalty is quiet, but it is real."

transitions.text: Quiet loyalty. I will take it.

states.text: A scarred watch veteran studies the road beyond the gate. "Plenty ask for an escort. Few ask what oath keeps a shield in place when the line breaks. If you want my trust, earn it."

params.text: The watch veteran values sworn duty over coin and tests commitment before agreeing to travel.

transitions.text: I will leave you to the wall.

states.text: "Stand the ugly watch. Keep your word when no one applauds it. Do that, and I listen. Ask for my shield before that, and the answer is no."

transitions.text: I understand the weight of an oath.
Then stand with me beyond the wall.

states.text: A harbor guard tightens a salt-stiff glove. "The docks teach patience. Anyone can bark orders. Few can keep frightened people moving when the tide and the knives turn together."

transitions.text: What sort of captain do you follow?

params.text: The harbor guard follows leaders who keep civilians steady under pressure and do not waste lives for pride.

states.text: "I follow the one who gets people home. Not the loudest voice. Not the fastest blade. Show me restraint when panic wants spectacle, and maybe I take your banner seriously."

transitions.text: Sail with me and keep that line intact.

states.text: A priest tends a ring of candles around a refugee shrine. "Mercy is easy when it is ceremonial. Harder when it costs sleep, blood, and the last clean bandage. If you ask me to travel, know what you are asking."

transitions.text: Tell me what that service demands.

params.text: The shrine priest serves only where hardship is real and promises are kept once the wounded start arriving.

transitions.text: Your shrine needs you more than I do.

states.text: "If I travel, it is to keep people alive, not to decorate a hero's story. Prove that you can carry burden without making others kneel to it, and I may walk beside you."

transitions.text: Then judge me by the burden I keep carrying.
Bring your light with us. We will need it.

states.text: A field healer crushes bitter herbs into a thick salve. "People ask for miracles. I ask whether they can hold a line long enough for medicine to matter. If you want me on the road, answer that first."

transitions.text: What answer are you looking for?

params.text: The field healer values discipline after the fight as much as courage during it.

transitions.text: Keep the salves ready. I may return.

states.text: "I travel with those who understand aftercare, restraint, and the cost of rushing the wounded back into danger. If that sounds slow to you, then do not ask again."

transitions.text: Victory means nothing if the wounded die later.
Come with us. We keep the living alive.
Then you know the long work.

states.text: A scout scratches route marks into a weathered board. "Fast feet are cheap. Returning with everyone alive is expensive. If you want me on your trail, convince me you understand the difference."

params.text: The scout follows leaders who know when to press and when to pull people back before the line collapses.

transitions.text: Keep your routes. I will keep mine.

states.text: "Show me a leader who does not confuse speed with judgment. Show me a commander who knows when guard beats aggressive and when neither matters because the trap is already sprung."

transitions.text: Discipline is faster than panic in the long run.
Then scout for us, and I will not waste what you find.
Good. Then maybe you listen as well as you command.

states.text: A fence counts marked coins into tidy stacks. "Everyone wants loyalty at cut-rate prices. I do not sell mine that cheaply. If you want me moving with your lot, prove you understand debt, leverage, and exits."

transitions.text: What makes a debt worth paying?

params.text: The fence respects leaders who know the price of favors and do not spend lives like loose coin.

states.text: "A good debt is one both sides remember the same way. Earn trust, keep your word, and leave me a clean road out if things sour. Do that, and I might call us even enough to travel."

transitions.text: Travel with us. I keep my accounts settled.
Then maybe we can do business on the road.

states.text: An ancient druid sits beneath a great oak, bark-like skin blending with the trunk.
The elder speaks of the old growth, how the roots connect every living thing in the forest. Certain herbs grow only where the ley lines converge.
The druid places a hand on the earth and a faint green glow pulses outward. You feel invigorated.
A hunched figure emerges from the mist, draped in moss and reeds. Fireflies orbit their staff.
The swamp holds many secrets beneath its murky waters. Not all who wander here find their way out, but the guide knows the safe paths.
The druid parts the fog with a gesture, revealing a hidden trail through the marshland.
A lone figure stands atop a windswept crag, communing with an eagle perched on their arm.
The hermit speaks of the mountain spirits, ancient beings of stone and wind that shape the peaks over millennia.
The druid calls upon the mountain wind, and loose stones arrange themselves into a protective cairn.
Luminescent fungi light the cavern where a druid tends to an underground garden of crystal and moss.
Deep beneath the surface, life persists in forms most never witness. The keeper nurtures fungal networks that carry whispers between the roots.
The druid offers a glowing mushroom that pulses with gentle warmth. Its light never fades.
A sun-weathered druid kneels beside a tiny oasis, coaxing water from cracked earth with quiet chanting.
The desert is not barren, merely patient. Seeds wait decades for a single rainfall. The nomad knows where water hides.
The druid traces a pattern in the sand, and a small spring bubbles up from below.
A druid stands where the waves meet the shore, salt spray turning to mist around their outstretched hands.
The tides carry messages from distant shores. The warden reads the currents and knows what approaches long before any lookout.
The druid speaks to the surf, and the waves deposit a cache of driftwood and useful flotsam at your feet.
In a shelter carved from blue ice, a druid peers into a frozen pool that reflects impossible starlight.
The seer reads futures in the patterns of frost. The cold preserves truth that warmth would distort.
The druid breathes onto the ice, and for a moment you see a vision of what lies ahead on your path.
In a cramped courtyard between tenements, a druid tends a vertical garden that defies the surrounding squalor.
Even in stone and iron, life finds a way. The gardener cultivates healing herbs that grow nowhere else in the city.
The druid clips a selection of herbs and wraps them in a broad leaf. The scent is surprisingly potent.
A druid adorned with feathers and living vines emerges from the canopy, accompanied by the calls of unseen creatures.
The jungle is the oldest teacher. Every vine, every insect, every predator holds a lesson for those who observe.
The shaman presses a hand to your forehead and suddenly the cacophony of jungle sounds resolves into distinct, meaningful voices.
A druid in a mask of woven roots stands at the edge of blighted land, burning corrupted plants with controlled flame.
Disease is nature unbalanced. The purifier restores equilibrium, even if it means burning away what cannot be saved.
The druid grinds a paste of rare herbs and applies it to your skin. A tingling warmth spreads through your body.
A vigilant druid patrols the border where the grove meets settled land, ensuring neither encroaches on the other.
The watcher mediates between civilization and wildness. Both have their place, but boundaries must be respected.
The druid marks your hand with a temporary sigil. The grove creatures will not trouble you.
Knee-deep in a rushing stream, a druid inspects the water with cupped hands, tasting it thoughtfully.
The river is the lifeblood of the region. Upstream disturbances always flow down. The tender monitors the health of all waters.
The druid purifies a waterskin with a murmured incantation and hands it back full of crystal-clear water.
A druid stands in an open field as dark clouds gather, arms raised, reading the approaching tempest.
Storms are not random fury. They are the sky communicating displeasure or renewal. The reader interprets the message.
The druid redirects a gust of wind, clearing the path ahead. A shaft of sunlight breaks through the clouds.
At the boundary between day and night, a druid performs a quiet ritual, balancing light and shadow.
Dawn and dusk are the thresholds. Magic flows most freely when the world is between states.
The druid captures a mote of twilight in a small vial and offers it to you. It glows with a soft purple light.
A druid sits surrounded by animals of every kind, from sparrows to wolves, all resting in unnatural peace.
Every creature has a voice. The caller listens and translates. Sometimes the beasts know dangers that humans miss.
The druid whistles a complex melody, and a hawk descends to perch on your shoulder, watching the horizon.
In a hollow beneath an ancient tree, a druid manipulates exposed roots like living rope, shaping them into useful forms.
Roots are the memory of the earth. They grow toward water, around stone, through anything given enough time.
The druid shapes a root into a sturdy walking staff and offers it. It feels warm and alive in your grip.
A druid in a damp underground chamber cultivates shelves of strange and colorful fungi, humming a low tune.
Fungi are the great recyclers. They break down the old to feed the new. Some varieties have remarkable properties.
The druid selects several mushrooms and packs them carefully in a pouch lined with damp cloth.
A druid monitors a controlled burn at the edge of a meadow, directing flames with precise gestures.
Fire is not the enemy of the forest. It clears the old growth so new life can emerge stronger.
The druid hands you a torch infused with druidic fire. It burns steadily but will not spread beyond your control.
Under the open sky, a young druid traces silver patterns in the air that linger like moonlight.
The moon governs transformation and cycles. Those who attune to its phases find their magic waxing with the light.
The druid touches your brow, and you feel a subtle awareness of the moon's current phase and its influence.
A druid presses an ear to the ground, listening intently, then rises with a knowing expression.
The land speaks to those who listen. Tremors, growth patterns, water flow, all tell a story of what has passed and what approaches.
The druid stamps the ground twice, and a faint map of underground passages appears traced in glowing lines on the soil.
An elderly monk sits in perfect stillness within a candlelit dojo, eyes closed but aware of every movement.
Discipline is not the absence of desire, but the mastery of it. The body follows where the mind leads.
The master demonstrates a fluid sequence of movements, then corrects your form with precise adjustments.
A barefoot monk rests beside the road, prayer beads clicking softly between weathered fingers.
Every step is a prayer, every road a teacher. The pilgrim seeks enlightenment not in temples but in the dust of the path.
The pilgrim shares a simple meal from their pack. The food is plain but fills you with unexpected warmth.
A muscular monk strikes a wooden training dummy with such speed that the impacts blur together.
Speed without control is just flailing. The trainer teaches precision, the art of placing each strike exactly where it must go.
The trainer guides you through a combat form, adjusting your balance and breathing with each repetition.
A figure detaches from the darkness so smoothly that you question whether the shadow itself just spoke.
Silence is not merely the absence of sound. It is a presence, a cloak, a weapon. The infiltrator walks between notice.
The monk shows you a breathing technique that slows your heartbeat and softens your footfalls.
A gaunt monk in threadbare robes sits atop a pile of discarded comforts, radiating an aura of profound peace.
Possessions anchor the spirit to the material. The sage has shed everything and found that nothing was lost, only clarity gained.
The sage touches your forehead, and for a fleeting moment the noise of the world falls away into pure silence.
A monk sways unsteadily, flask in hand, but their eyes are sharp and their stance deceptively balanced.
Predictability is the enemy. The drunken style turns chaos into advantage, making the fighter impossible to read.
The monk stumbles toward you in what seems like a fall, but somehow you end up on the ground instead.
A serene monk kneels beside an injured traveler, pressing specific points along their body with practiced precision.
The body knows how to heal itself. The medic merely reminds it, unblocking the flow of vital energy.
The monk presses two fingers to your wrist and a warm current flows through your body, easing aches you had forgotten.
A monk stands in a pool of sunlight, palms radiating a soft golden glow that flickers like living flame.
The sun is discipline made manifest. It rises without fail, gives without asking, burns away corruption. The guardian channels this constancy.
The monk cups their hands and offers you a small orb of warm light. It hovers above your palm, steady and unwavering.
A pale monk in dark wrappings sits before a collection of memorial stones, maintaining a vigil with absolute calm.
Death is not an enemy. It is a threshold. The reaper eases the passage and ensures the dead find peace.
The monk touches a memorial stone, and a faint presence seems to stir, then settle with a whispered sigh of release.
A monk moves through a kata that shifts fluidly between four distinct styles, each evocative of a different element.
North wind brings endurance, south brings passion, east brings clarity, west brings intuition. The complete warrior commands all four.
The monk demonstrates a technique that channels a burst of elemental energy through a palm strike.
A monk stands guard at the monastery entrance, serene but unmistakably vigilant.
The monastery accepts all seekers, but demands respect. Weapons of war remain outside these walls.
The monk steps aside and gestures you through the gate with a slight bow.
A monk sits in the center of a sand garden, raking patterns with a small wooden tool.
The patterns in the sand mirror the patterns in the mind. By ordering one, you order the other.
The monk hands you the rake and gestures to an unmarked section of sand. You find the act surprisingly calming.
A young monk bounces lightly on their feet in a training yard, clearly eager for a practice bout.
Theory without practice is empty form. The sparring partner offers a safe space to test techniques against a skilled opponent.
You exchange a series of controlled blows. The monk nods approvingly at your form and suggests adjustments.
A stout monk tends a collection of clay pots, the air rich with the scent of fermenting herbs and grain.
Brewing is meditation in liquid form. Patience, attention, and respect for the ingredients yield something greater than the sum.
The brewmaster offers you a cup of warm herbal brew. It tastes complex and fortifying.
A monk stands in a bell tower, waiting for the precise moment to toll the hour.
The bells mark time for the monastery. Each ring reminds us that moments pass and will not return. Use them well.
The monk strikes the bell at the exact moment of the hour. The resonance fills your chest with a sense of purpose.
A monk carefully unrolls an ancient scroll, copying its contents with painstaking precision onto fresh parchment.
Knowledge is fragile. Paper decays, ink fades. The keeper ensures that wisdom survives by faithfully reproducing it.
The monk transcribes a short passage and hands you the copy. It describes a breathing exercise for focus.
A monk chops vegetables with a rhythmic precision that transforms cooking into a kind of moving meditation.
Preparing food is an act of service. Each cut, each seasoning decision, is made with awareness and care.
The monk sets a bowl of simple but perfectly balanced soup before you. Every ingredient complements the others.
A monk prunes a bonsai tree with delicate snips, each cut revealing a more refined form within.
The tree already knows its perfect shape. The gardener merely removes what is unnecessary.
The monk offers you a small cutting from the bonsai, wrapped in damp cloth. It seems to radiate calm.
A monk maintains a small wayside shrine, replacing candles and clearing debris from the path.
Pilgrims need landmarks. The keeper ensures that those who walk the path find comfort and direction at each waypoint.
The monk marks your map with the locations of other shrines along your route, adding notes about nearby water sources.
A monk grinds ink with a stone pestle, producing a pigment so dark it seems to absorb light.
The quality of the ink determines the permanence of the word. What is worth writing is worth writing well.
The monk fills a small vial with their finest ink and seals it with wax. The ink seems to shimmer faintly.
A cloaked figure sits behind a desk covered in contracts written in languages that shift when you look away.
Every pact has terms. The broker ensures both parties understand the price. Power is never free, but it can be fairly traded.
The broker draws up a minor contract and offers it. The terms are clear: a small service in exchange for useful knowledge.
A warlock lounges in a dark corner, shadows pooling unnaturally around their feet like obedient pets.
Shadows remember everything they touch. The whisperer listens to their stories and trades in secrets that daylight cannot reveal.
The warlock whispers to the shadows, which ripple and then present a dark crystal containing a useful secret.
A warlock with wildflowers growing from their hair moves in a strange, captivating dance that bends the light around them.
The fey courts are beautiful and terrible. The dancer channels their patron's grace, making the impossible seem effortless and the mundane magical.
The dancer traces a sigil in the air that transforms into a small fey charm, glittering with otherworldly light.
A warlock sits rigidly, dark veins visible beneath pale skin, an air of barely contained power crackling around them.
The fiend grants power without hesitation. The price comes later, always later, and always higher than expected. But the bound one endures.
The warlock channels a burst of infernal energy into a small stone, which glows with a dark red light.
A warlock stares into empty space, occasionally flinching at things only they can perceive.
The patron speaks in concepts that language cannot contain. Understanding comes in fragments, each one reshaping the mind that receives it.
The warlock touches your temple. For an instant, you perceive a vast, alien awareness turning its attention toward you, then away.
A warlock draws a blade that seems to drink the surrounding light, its edge humming with spectral energy.
The weapon is the pact, and the pact is the weapon. The duelist's blade is an extension of their patron's will.
The duelist offers a brief sparring demonstration. Their blade moves with an intelligence of its own.
A warlock radiates a soft golden light, their pact a paradox of heavenly power channeled through mortal ambition.
Not all patrons lurk in darkness. The beacon draws power from a celestial entity who values the warlock's determination.
The warlock channels healing light through their hands. The warmth is genuine but carries an underlying sense of obligation.
A warlock pores over ancient texts, their eyes carrying the weight of experiences far beyond their apparent age.
The undying patron offers perspective. Centuries of memory flow through the pact, granting wisdom but also a haunting sense of loss.
The warlock shares a fragment of their patron's memory. You see a place as it was centuries ago, rich with forgotten knowledge.
A warlock stands on a high point, lightning arcing between their fingers as dark clouds swirl overhead.
The storm is alive and hungry. The caller channels its fury, directing it with will alone. It is exhilarating and terrifying in equal measure.
The warlock releases a controlled bolt that strikes a distant dead tree, shattering it. The air smells of ozone.
A warlock reads from a book that writes itself, new pages appearing as fast as old ones are consumed.
The Book of Shadows contains everything the patron knows that can be expressed in words. Reading it is a lifelong pursuit.
The scholar copies a useful passage from their tome and offers it. The ink shimmers with eldritch light.
A warlock stands at a crossroads, officiating transactions between desperate mortals and unseen entities.
Every deal needs a witness. The pact witness ensures that terms are met and neither party cheats the other.
The witness reviews your situation and offers advice on negotiating with supernatural entities.
A warlock inscribes protective glyphs on a doorframe, each symbol flaring briefly before settling into the wood.
Curses are real and common. The ward maker provides protection for those who cannot protect themselves.
The warlock inscribes a small ward on a token and offers it to you. It tingles faintly against your skin.
A warlock tends to a menagerie of small supernatural creatures, each one a familiar bound to a different pact.
Familiars are partners, not pets. The handler teaches proper care and communication between warlock and companion.
One of the familiars, a tiny shadow cat, sniffs your hand and purrs. The handler notes this with interest.
A warlock copies eldritch formulae onto scrolls using ink that glows faintly in patterns that resist direct observation.
The scribe transcribes power into portable form. Each scroll contains a single invocation, usable by anyone willing to speak its words.
The scribe offers a completed scroll with careful instructions for its use.
A warlock reclines on a pile of cushions, their breathing slow and rhythmic, a faint astral shimmer around their form.
The dream walker navigates the sleeping minds of the willing, helping resolve nightmares and extracting lost memories.
The warlock guides you through a brief meditation. Forgotten details from a recent memory surface with surprising clarity.
A warlock works within a complex circle of containment sigils, carefully adjusting each line with chalk and silver dust.
Summoning without binding is suicide. The specialist ensures that whatever answers the call remains within controlled parameters.
The specialist reviews your knowledge of protective circles and offers corrections that could save your life.
A warlock mediates between a confused petitioner and an entity that speaks only in cryptic visions.
Patrons rarely communicate in ways mortals find intuitive. The interpreter bridges that gap, translating alien intent into actionable guidance.
The interpreter helps you understand a recurring dream. The symbols resolve into a clear, if unsettling, message.
A warlock maintains a ledger of soul-debts with meticulous precision, each entry balanced to the letter.
Souls are currency in certain circles. The accountant ensures that debts are tracked accurately and paid fairly.
The accountant reviews your spiritual balance and notes no outstanding debts. A rare state, they observe.
A warlock stares into a perfectly smooth black mirror. Occasionally, something in the mirror stares back.
Beyond the material plane lies the void, not empty but full of things that have no name. The gazer observes without being consumed.
The warlock angles the mirror so you catch a glimpse. For an instant, you see impossible geometries before looking away.
A warlock commands a retinue of bound spirits that manifest as faintly visible servants performing mundane tasks.
The chain of command is literal. Each spirit serves willingly within the terms of their binding. Force breeds rebellion.
One of the bound spirits approaches you and offers a small service, mending a tear in your clothing with spectral thread.
A sorcerer laughs nervously as sparks of random color leap from their fingertips. Nearby objects briefly levitate.
Wild magic is untamed potential. Every spell could do exactly what is intended, or something completely unexpected. That is the thrill.
The sorcerer attempts to conjure a simple light. Instead, a burst of harmless butterflies explodes outward. They shrug cheerfully.
Faint scales shimmer along the sorcerer's arms, and their eyes carry the vertical pupils of a dragon.
Dragon blood flows through certain lineages. The heir channels this ancient power, their magic carrying the weight of draconic will.
The sorcerer exhales a controlled stream of elemental energy that scorches the ground in a precise line.
The air around this sorcerer crackles with static. Their hair floats in a permanent halo of electrical charge.
Storm sorcerers are born during tempests. The weather lives inside them, always churning, always seeking release.
The sorcerer raises a hand and a miniature thunderstorm forms in their palm, complete with tiny lightning bolts.
A sorcerer moves through a dimly lit room, their shadow moving independently, sometimes arriving at destinations before they do.
Shadow magic is innate for those touched by the Shadowfell. Darkness is not the absence of light, it is its own substance.
The sorcerer steps into a patch of shadow and emerges from another across the room. The transition is seamless.
A sorcerer kneels in prayer, but the light that surrounds them comes from within, not from any deity's altar.
Divine power usually flows through faith and devotion. For the chosen, it flows through blood. The gods marked their lineage long ago.
The sorcerer channels a wave of healing warmth that mends a nearby wounded animal without any visible casting.
A sorcerer with faintly glowing skin and hair that flickers like flame stands amid a circle of ash that was recently their campfire.
Death is a temporary inconvenience for those carrying the phoenix spark. The reborn has already died twice and returned each time.
The sorcerer deliberately burns their own hand, then watches as the wound closes in seconds, leaving no mark.
A sorcerer sits slightly apart from others, their presence causing mild unease. Objects near them occasionally move without cause.
Contact with the Far Realm leaves marks. The touched carry alien power that bends reality in ways that defy conventional magic.
The sorcerer focuses on a stone, which lifts, splits into four pieces, and rearranges into an impossible geometric shape.
A stocky sorcerer stands with feet planted firmly, their skin carrying a faint granite texture when the light hits right.
Earth answers those born of its deep places. The warden draws strength from stone itself, becoming as unyielding as bedrock.
The sorcerer stamps a foot and a wall of stone erupts from the ground, then slowly retracts at their command.
A sorcerer with blue-tinted skin and the faint scent of ocean salt manipulates a floating sphere of seawater.
The ocean's power is in its persistence. The tide sorcerer channels this relentless force, wearing down all resistance.
The sorcerer directs the water sphere to encircle a practice target, applying crushing pressure with fluid grace.
A sorcerer with eyes that click and whir like mechanisms studies a complex pattern of interlocking magical formulae.
Order underlies chaos. The clockwork sorcerer perceives the mathematical foundations of magic and adjusts variables with precision.
The sorcerer traces a geometric spell pattern in the air. Each angle is exact, and the resulting effect is perfectly calibrated.
A sorcerer examines travelers with a monocle that reveals hidden magical lineages in their blood.
Many carry latent sorcerous blood without knowing it. The tester identifies potential and advises on awakening or suppression.
The sorcerer peers at you through the monocle and nods thoughtfully, making notes in a leather journal.
A sorcerer maintains a containment circle where wild magic surges are safely discharged into absorbing crystals.
Uncontrolled surges are dangerous to everyone nearby. The handler provides a safe outlet for sorcerers struggling with volatile power.
The handler guides you through a controlled release exercise. Excess magical energy flows safely into a waiting crystal.
A sorcerer displays an array of small crystals, each containing a different metamagic technique in crystallized form.
Metamagic allows the shaping of raw magical power. The dealer provides techniques that enhance spellcasting flexibility.
The dealer selects a crystal and cracks it open. A brief flash of insight about magical manipulation floods your mind.
A sorcerer shapes raw magical energy into crystalline focusing devices, each one humming with contained potential.
Innate magic benefits from focus. The crafter produces tools that help sorcerers channel their power more efficiently.
The crafter hands you a small crystal attuned to your specific magical resonance. It vibrates softly in your palm.
A sorcerer maintains a vast genealogical chart tracing sorcerous bloodlines across generations and continents.
Understanding where your power comes from helps you understand where it can go. Lineage shapes the nature of innate magic.
The historian traces your potential lineage on the chart, connecting dots between families and regions.
A sorcerer patrols the perimeter of an area where reality flickers and shifts, keeping curious travelers at safe distance.
Wild magic zones occur where the weave is thin or damaged. The warden prevents the unprepared from wandering into danger.
The warden marks safe paths through the zone on your map and provides a charm that stabilizes minor fluctuations.
A sorcerer facilitates exchanges of magical energy between those who have surplus and those who need more.
Raw magical potential can be shared, transferred, or stored. The broker manages these transactions fairly.
The broker evaluates your current magical reserves and suggests an arrangement that would benefit your journey.
A sorcerer works with a student whose hands keep involuntarily producing frost, coaching them to control the output.
New sorcerers often manifest power before they can control it. The coach helps channel raw ability into deliberate action.
The coach observes your magical tendencies and offers targeted exercises to improve your control.
A sorcerer sits at the center of four elemental symbols, cycling through fire, water, earth, and air manifestations.
Every sorcerer has a natural elemental affinity. The attuner helps identify and strengthen that connection.
The attuner guides you through an attunement ritual. One of the four symbols glows brighter in your presence.
A sorcerer hammers pure magical energy into physical form on an anvil made of compressed starlight.
Catalysts amplify innate power. The smith forges these devices from raw magic, creating tools unique to each sorcerer.
The smith creates a small catalyst tuned to your magical signature. It pulses in sync with your heartbeat.
A river crossing guard leans against the toll gate, watching the bridge traffic with practiced eyes.
The crossing sees hundreds of travelers daily. The guard watches for smugglers, fugitives, and those who would damage the bridge.
The guard stamps your travel pass and waves you through to the bridge.
A merchant displays goods salvaged from river wreckage alongside imported trade wares.
River trade brings goods from upstream and downstream settlements. Wreckage from rapids yields surprising treasures.
The merchant wraps your purchase in oilcloth to protect it from the ever-present river spray.
The innkeeper of the Rushing Waters tavern wipes down a bar made from reclaimed river wood.
The inn caters to travelers waiting for safe crossing conditions. During flood season, guests sometimes stay for weeks.
The innkeeper prepares a room with a view of the river. The sound of rushing water is constant but oddly soothing.
A blacksmith works near the waterwheel that powers the forge bellows, the rhythmic splash keeping time.
The river powers the forge. The smith specializes in tools for bridge maintenance and boat repair.
The smith sharpens your blade using the river-powered grinding wheel. The edge is keen and true.
A healer tends to a patient suffering from river fever, applying cool compresses with practiced care.
River diseases are common here. The healer knows dozens of waterborne ailments and their remedies.
The healer provides a bundle of dried herbs that ward off the most common river maladies.
A priest maintains a shrine to the river spirits, offering daily blessings for safe passage.
The river is both provider and destroyer. The priest mediates between the settlement and the water's unpredictable moods.
The priest blesses a small stone and asks you to drop it in the river as you cross for safe passage.
A sage studies river flow patterns from a tower overlooking the crossing, charts and instruments everywhere.
The river's behavior predicts weather, upstream events, and seasonal changes. The sage reads these signs for the town.
The sage shares observations about upstream conditions that will affect your route.
A fisherman mends nets on the riverbank, watching the current with the patience of long experience.
Life at the crossing revolves around the river. Fishing, ferrying, bridge tolls, and salvage are the main occupations.
The fisherman points out the best fording points for those who cannot afford the bridge toll.
The mayor reviews bridge maintenance reports, the weight of infrastructure responsibility evident in their expression.
The crossing exists because of the bridge. The mayor balances toll revenue, maintenance costs, and the competing demands of traders.
The mayor grants you passage priority, noting your party could be useful to the settlement.
A scout wades ashore from the shallows, carrying reports about conditions along the river upstream.
Scouts monitor the river for flooding, debris, and hostile activity. Early warning saves lives at the crossing.
The scout marks your map with current river conditions and warns about an unstable section of the bank ahead.
A stablehand leads a nervous horse across the narrow bridge, speaking softly to calm the animal.
Animals dislike the bridge. The vibration and sound of rushing water spook most horses on first crossing.
The stablehand blindfolds your mount for the crossing and leads it across with expert calm.
A cook stirs a massive pot of fish stew, the aroma competing with the smell of the river.
River fish are the staple diet here. The cook knows forty ways to prepare them and is working on number forty-one.
The cook ladles out a generous portion of stew. Fresh fish, river herbs, and a hint of something unexpected.
A cliff city guard patrols a narrow ledge path, seemingly unbothered by the dizzying drop beside them.
Vertigo is the first thing new guards must overcome. The second is learning to fight on a three-foot-wide path.
The guard clips a safety line to your belt and escorts you along the exposed section of the path.
A merchant operates from a shop carved into the cliff face, goods displayed on shelves cut into living rock.
Space is at a premium in the cliff city. Every niche and shelf is utilized. Goods that are lightweight and compact sell best.
The merchant wraps your purchase tightly and suggests the best way to carry it on the vertical paths.
The innkeeper of the Eagle's Perch gestures to a room with a doorway that opens onto a sheer drop and a stunning view.
Most rooms have no windows, just openings in the cliff face. The view is spectacular. The safety ropes are mandatory.
The innkeeper provides a room with anchor points for your gear and a heavy curtain against the wind.
A blacksmith works in a forge built into a natural chimney in the cliff, smoke venting straight upward.
The cliff provides natural ventilation for the forge. The smith specializes in climbing gear, pitons, and lightweight tools.
The smith inspects your equipment and replaces a worn carabiner, refusing payment for something safety-critical.
A healer sets a broken arm, an injury all too common in a city built on vertical surfaces.
Falls are the leading cause of injury. The healer keeps splints, ropes, and rescue equipment alongside medicines.
The healer provides a salve for altitude sickness and vertigo, common ailments for visitors.
A priest tends a shrine carved into the highest accessible point of the cliff, open to the sky above.
The cliff city was founded by those who sought to be closer to the heavens. The priest maintains that original vision.
The priest offers a blessing for sure-footedness, touching your boots and whispering a prayer.
A sage studies the geological layers exposed in the cliff face, each stratum revealing a different age.
The cliff is a library written in stone. Each layer tells a story of what the world was like when it was deposited.
The sage points out a fossil in the wall and explains its significance to the region's history.
A cliff city resident carries supplies up a rope ladder with practiced ease, barely pausing for breath.
Everyone here climbs. Children learn to use safety ropes before they learn to read. It is simply how life works.
The resident shares tips for navigating the vertical paths and warns about a rope bridge under repair.
The mayor's office is the largest carved chamber in the cliff, its window a wide opening framing the valley below.
Governing a vertical city requires thinking in three dimensions. Zoning includes not just area but altitude.
The mayor provides an official pass that allows access to the restricted upper levels of the city.
A scout descends on a rappelling line, landing lightly on the ledge with a report in hand.
Cliff scouts are the best climbers in the city. They monitor the cliff face for rockfalls and intruders.
The scout shows you a faster route up the cliff face that avoids the crowded main paths.
A stablehand tends mountain goats rather than horses in a stable carved into a wide ledge.
Horses cannot navigate the cliff city. Mountain goats carry loads up paths no horse could manage.
The stablehand assigns a sure-footed goat to carry your heavier supplies up the vertical paths.
A cook prepares meals in a kitchen with a view that most fine restaurants would envy, perched on a clifftop ledge.
Cooking at altitude and on narrow surfaces requires special techniques. Heavy pots are anchored in place.
The cook serves a meal of dried meats, hard cheese, and trail bread designed for eating on narrow ledges.
An oasis guard stands in the shade of a palm, watching the approaching caravan road through a heat shimmer.
Water rights are strictly enforced. The guard ensures no one takes more than their allotment from the oasis.
The guard issues you a water token, good for three days of oasis access.
A merchant spreads silks and spices across a colorful tent, the scents mixing with the dry desert air.
The oasis market sits at the crossroads of four major trade routes. Goods from every corner pass through here.
The merchant offers a fair price, explaining that competition at the oasis keeps margins thin but quality high.
The innkeeper of the Desert Rose caravanserai greets you from behind a counter cooled by clay jug evaporation.
The caravanserai provides shade, water, and stabling for caravan beasts. During peak season, space must be booked days in advance.
The innkeeper assigns you a cool room on the north side of the building, shaded from the worst sun.
A blacksmith works under an awning, the forge heat competing with the desert sun.
Desert conditions demand special metallurgy. The smith treats blades to resist sand abrasion and heat warping.
The smith applies a sand-resistant coating to your weapon and tightens any loose fittings.
A healer stores remedies in clay pots buried in wet sand to keep them cool and effective.
Heatstroke, dehydration, and sandstorm injuries are the common complaints. The healer also treats camel and horse ailments.
The healer provides salt tablets and a cooling compress technique for desert travel.
A priest maintains a water shrine at the oasis edge, performing purification rituals at dawn and dusk.
Water is sacred in the desert. The priest ensures the oasis remains pure and thanks the spirits for its continued flow.
The priest fills a blessed waterskin from the shrine's pool. It stays cool far longer than ordinary water.
A sage charts the movement of desert sands, predicting which trade routes will be passable each season.
The desert is not static. Dunes shift, oases dry up or appear, and landmarks vanish under sand. The sage tracks all of it.
The sage provides updated route information and warns about a sandstorm system approaching from the south.
A water carrier balances clay jugs on a shoulder yoke, distributing the day's allotment to market stalls.
Water carriers are essential to the oasis economy. Without them, market operations would halt within hours.
The commoner shares local gossip about a caravan that arrived with news of a discovered ruin to the east.
The oasis mayor adjudicates a water dispute between two caravan masters in an open-air court shaded by palms.
Water allocation is the mayor's primary concern. Every dispute eventually comes back to water rights.
The mayor welcomes you to the oasis and extends trading privileges for the duration of your stay.
A desert scout rides in on a sand-colored horse, robes and face wrap caked in dust.
Scouts range far into the desert, monitoring trade routes for bandits, sandstorms, and changes in terrain.
The scout reports that the northern route is clear but recommends extra water for the eastern path.
A stablehand waters camels and horses at separate troughs, managing the animals with quiet authority.
Camels and horses do not mix well. The stablehand keeps them separated and ensures all animals receive adequate water.
The stablehand evaluates your mount and recommends rest before the next leg of desert travel.
A cook tends a clay oven, producing flatbread and roasted meats seasoned with desert spices.
Desert cuisine relies on preservation. Spices, salt, and drying keep food edible in the heat. Fresh ingredients are a luxury.
The cook serves a platter of spiced lamb, flatbread, and dates. The flavors are intense and satisfying.
A guard patrols with a lantern, though the bioluminescent glow provides enough light to see by.
Tunnels branch in every direction. The guard prevents unauthorized access to the deeper levels.
The guard marks your passage permit and directs you to the main cavern market.
A merchant displays goods under the blue glow of luminescent crystals, the light giving everything an ethereal quality.
Underground goods include rare minerals, fungal products, and items from deep-dwelling creatures. Surface goods command premium prices.
The merchant wraps your purchase in cave-silk, a strong material produced by underground spiders.
The innkeeper of the Glowing Grotto tends a bar carved from a single massive stalagmite.
Time is strange underground. Without sun or stars, the inn keeps its own schedule based on fungal bloom cycles.
The innkeeper shows you to a room with walls studded with softly glowing crystals. It is surprisingly warm.
A blacksmith works with underground metals, the forge built beside a natural lava vent that provides heat.
Deep ores have unique properties. The smith forges alloys not possible with surface metals.
The smith examines your weapon and offers to reinforce it with a trace of deep-iron for added durability.
A healer cultivates medicinal fungi in tiered gardens, each species requiring different amounts of moisture and darkness.
Underground medicine relies heavily on fungi and mineral compounds. The healer knows which growths cure and which kill.
The healer provides a fungal poultice that promotes rapid healing in the damp underground conditions.
A priest tends a shrine where natural crystal formations have been arranged into a pattern of worship.
The deep earth has its own spirits. The priest honors them, ensuring the caverns remain stable and the glow continues.
The priest touches a crystal that chimes softly, and you feel a sense of groundedness and protection.
A sage maps the cavern system, which extends far beyond the settled areas into unexplored darkness.
The underground city occupies perhaps a tenth of the known cavern network. What lies beyond is still being discovered.
The sage shares information about a recently discovered passage that may lead to a new crystal deposit.
A fungus farmer tends rows of cultivated mushrooms, adjusting moisture levels with a fine spray.
Most food in the underground city comes from fungal cultivation. Different species serve as bread, meat, and vegetables.
The farmer shares a sample of a new cultivar. It tastes surprisingly like roasted nuts.
The mayor's chamber is a natural amphitheater with perfect acoustics, allowing governance by public address.
Underground governance requires consensus. Sound carries far in the caverns, and disputes echo longer than anyone wants.
The mayor grants you access to the lower markets and assigns a guide for the less-traveled tunnels.
A scout emerges from a narrow crevice, covered in mineral dust, carrying samples from a deep survey.
Underground scouts explore new tunnels, check for structural weaknesses, and map water sources.
The scout warns about a recently discovered sinkhole on the eastern route and marks a detour on your map.
A stablehand manages a pen of large, pale pack lizards bred for underground hauling.
Horses cannot navigate underground. The lizards are sure-footed in darkness and can climb walls when needed.
The stablehand assigns a docile pack lizard to your party, explaining its care requirements.
A cook stirs a pot over a geothermal vent, the natural heat serving as an eternal stove.
Underground cooking uses geothermal heat and fungal ingredients. The results are unique and often surprising.
The cook serves a bowl of cave-mushroom stew with mineral-water broth. The flavor is earthy and rich.
A port guard watches the harbor from a watchtower, noting the flags and conditions of incoming vessels.
The port guard identifies ships by their rigging and flags. Pirates disguise themselves, but details always betray them.
The guard clears your party for shore leave and provides a map of approved areas.
A merchant unloads exotic goods from a recently docked ship, tropical fruits and rare shells among them.
Island trade deals in what the sea provides and what the jungle yields. Coral, pearl, rare wood, and spices flow outward.
The merchant offers tropical goods at fair prices, noting that a storm delayed the usual supply ship.
The innkeeper of the Anchor and Compass runs a breezy establishment with open walls and palm-thatch roof.
Sailors, traders, and adventurers all pass through. The innkeeper hears everything and shares selectively.
The innkeeper sets up hammocks in a room cooled by cross-breezes from the harbor.
A blacksmith specializes in ship fittings and marine hardware, the forge built to resist the salt air.
Salt water corrodes everything. The smith treats all metal with protective coatings and repairs storm-damaged fittings.
The smith coats your weapons with a salt-resistant treatment that will last several weeks.
A healer treats a sailor for tropical fever, applying cool compresses and administering bark extract.
Tropical diseases are diverse and aggressive. The healer maintains a pharmacy of remedies from dozens of islands.
The healer provides preventative doses for the most common tropical ailments.
A priest maintains a shrine overlooking the harbor, offering prayers for safe voyages and fair winds.
The sea is fickle. The priest asks for calm waters and warns when the omens suggest storms.
The priest ties a blessed knot in a cord and gives it to you. Sailors say such knots control the wind.
A sage studies nautical charts and weather patterns, advising ship captains on optimal departure times.
Currents, winds, and tides follow patterns. The sage reads them and reduces the risk of ocean travel.
The sage provides detailed sailing directions to your destination, including alternate routes for different wind conditions.
A dockworker loads cargo with practiced efficiency, calling out weights and destinations in a sing-song rhythm.
The docks never sleep. Ships arrive and depart at all hours, and the workers shift to match the tides.
The dockworker points out which ships are hiring crew and which captains have reputations worth avoiding.
The port mayor conducts business from a balcony overlooking the harbor, ship manifests stacked beside a telescope.
The port's prosperity depends on trade volume and security. The mayor balances merchant needs with naval defense.
The mayor offers your party reduced docking fees in exchange for a report on conditions along your route.
A scout arrives by small boat, having surveyed nearby islands for pirate activity.
Island scouts patrol the archipelago, mapping uncharted islets and watching for hostile vessels.
The scout reports that the southern passage is clear but recommends avoiding the northern route due to pirate sightings.
A stablehand manages a corral of donkeys used for hauling cargo from the docks to the upper town.
Horses are rare on the island. Donkeys handle the steep paths between the harbor and the hilltop better.
The stablehand provides a donkey to carry your supplies up the steep path to the town center.
A cook grills freshly caught fish over coconut shell coals, the aroma drawing a queue of hungry sailors.
Island cuisine features seafood, tropical fruit, and spices that grow nowhere else. Every island has its signature dish.
The cook serves grilled fish with mango relish and a side of spiced rice. The freshness is unmistakable.
A volcano forge guard wears heat-resistant armor and monitors the lava vents that serve as natural defenses.
The caldera is naturally fortified. The guard watches the few cooled paths that serve as entrances.
The guard tests the ground temperature near the path and confirms it is safe to cross.
A merchant sells obsidian crafts, heat-resistant fabrics, and minerals found only in volcanic soil.
Volcanic products are prized across the land. Obsidian blades, pumice stone, and mineral dyes fetch high prices.
The merchant presents a selection of volcanic glass items, each one sharp and beautiful.
The innkeeper manages a lodge built over a hot spring, steam rising through vents in the stone floor.
The volcano keeps the settlement warm year-round. The hot springs are the main attraction for visitors.
The innkeeper offers a room with a private hot spring bath. The mineral-rich water soothes aching muscles.
A blacksmith works beside a lava channel, using the natural heat to maintain perfect forging temperatures.
No bellows needed here. The volcano provides endless heat. The smith focuses entirely on technique and metallurgy.
